From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Alcoholic beverage

Not to be confused with [Liquor](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") or [Lacquer](/wiki/Lacquer "Lacquer").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Benedictine_01_08.jpg/250px-Benedictine_01_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Benedictine_01_08.jpg)

[Bénédictine](/wiki/B%C3%A9n%C3%A9dictine "Bénédictine"), a traditional French herbal liqueur

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Licor-Beir%C3%A3o-%26-Orus_-_Orus_Clothing_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Licor-Beir%C3%A3o-%26-Orus_-_Orus_Clothing_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Licor-Beir%C3%A3o-%26-Orus_-_Orus_Clothing_(cropped).png)

[Licor Beirão](/wiki/Licor_Beir%C3%A3o "Licor Beirão"), a traditional Portuguese spice liqueur

A **liqueur** ([UK](/wiki/British_English "British English"): [/lɪˈkjʊər/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Liqueur.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Liqueur.wav") [*li-KURE*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/lɪˈkɜːr/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*li-KUR*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[1]](#cite_note-1) French: [[likœʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is an [alcoholic drink](/wiki/Alcoholic_drink "Alcoholic drink") composed of [spirits](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") (often [rectified spirit](/wiki/Rectified_spirit "Rectified spirit")) and additional flavorings such as [sugar](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), [fruits](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit"), [herbs](/wiki/Herb "Herb"), and [spices](/wiki/Spice "Spice"). Often served with or after [dessert](/wiki/Dessert "Dessert"), they are typically heavily sweetened and un-aged, beyond a resting period during production, when necessary, for their flavors to mingle.

Liqueurs are historical descendants of [herbal medicines](/wiki/Herbal_medicine "Herbal medicine"). They were made in [France](/wiki/France "France") as early as the 13th century, often prepared by [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") (for example, [Chartreuse](/wiki/Chartreuse_(liqueur) "Chartreuse (liqueur)")). Today they are produced all over the world, commonly served neat, over ice, with [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), in [cocktails](/wiki/Cocktails "Cocktails"), and used in [cooking](/wiki/Cooking "Cooking").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The French word *liqueur* is derived from the Latin *liquifacere*, which means "to dissolve".[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In some parts of the United States and Canada, liqueurs may be referred to as cordials, or [schnapps](/wiki/Schnapps "Schnapps").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) This can cause confusion as in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") a [cordial](/wiki/Squash_(drink) "Squash (drink)") would refer to a non-alcoholic concentrated fruit syrup, typically diluted to taste and consumed as a non-carbonated [soft drink](/wiki/Soft_drink "Soft drink").[[6]](#cite_note-se-6) Schnapps, on the other hand, can refer to any distilled beverage in Germany and [aquavit](/wiki/Akvavit "Akvavit") in Scandinavian countries.[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-7)

## Legal definitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Legal definitions")]

In the United States, where spirits are often called "liquor", there is often confusion discerning between liqueurs and [liquors](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor"), due to the many different types of flavored spirits that are available today (e.g., [flavored vodka](/wiki/Vodka_infusion "Vodka infusion")). Liqueurs generally contain a lower [alcohol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") content (15–30% [ABV](/wiki/ABV "ABV")) than spirits[[8]](#cite_note-8) and have a sweetener mixed, while some can have an ABV as high as 55%.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Canada")]

Under the Food and Drug Regulations (C.R.C., c. 870), liqueurs are produced from mixing alcohol with plant materials.[[10]](#cite_note-CRCc870-10) These materials include juices or extracts from fruits, flowers, leaves or other plant materials.[[10]](#cite_note-CRCc870-10) The extracts are obtained by soaking, filtering or softening the plant substances. A sweetening agent should be added in an amount that is at least 2.5 percent of the finished liqueur. The alcohol percentage shall be at least 23%.[[10]](#cite_note-CRCc870-10) It may also contain natural or artificial flavoring and color.[[10]](#cite_note-CRCc870-10)

### European Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: European Union")]

The European Union directive on [spirit drinks](/wiki/Liquor "Liquor") provides guidelines applicable to all liqueurs.[[11]](#cite_note-directive-11) As such, a liqueur must

* contain a minimum of 15% [alcohol by volume](/wiki/Alcohol_by_volume "Alcohol by volume"),
* in most cases, contain sweetening equivalent to at least 100 grams of [inverted sugar](/wiki/Inverted_sugar_syrup "Inverted sugar syrup") per liter,[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* be created using [neutral grain alcohol](/wiki/Rectified_spirit#European_Union "Rectified spirit") and/or [distillate(s) of agricultural origin](/wiki/Liquor#Distillate_of_agricultural_origin "Liquor"),
* be flavored with natural, or nature-identical, flavorings, and
* be labeled with the alcohol content and a list of any [food colorings](/wiki/Food_coloring "Food coloring").

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

After the UK formally left The European Union on the 31st January 2020, many existing EU laws rolled over into the UK framework including the legalities around Liqueurs and since have not significantly changed. [[13]](#cite_note-13)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: United States")]

The [Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau](/wiki/Alcohol_and_Tobacco_Tax_and_Trade_Bureau "Alcohol and Tobacco Tax and Trade Bureau") regulates liqueurs similarly to Canada. Liqueurs (and also cordials) are defined as products created by mixing or redistilling distilled spirits with fruit, plant products, natural flavors, extracts, or sweeteners. These additives must be added in an amount not less than 2.5% by weight of the final product.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Preparation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Preparation")]

Some liqueurs are prepared by [infusing](/wiki/Infusion "Infusion") certain woods, fruits, or [flowers](/wiki/Flower "Flower") in either water or [alcohol](/wiki/Ethanol "Ethanol") and adding sugar or other items. Others are [distilled](/wiki/Distillation "Distillation") from [aromatic](/wiki/Aroma "Aroma") or flavoring agents.

[Anise](/wiki/Anise "Anise") and [Rakı](/wiki/Rak%C4%B1 "Rakı") liqueurs have the property of turning from transparent to cloudy when added to water: the oil of anise remains in solution in the presence of a high concentration of alcohol, but coalesces when the alcohol concentration is reduced; this is known as the [ouzo effect](/wiki/Ouzo_effect "Ouzo effect").[[15]](#cite_note-Sanchez-Dominguez_Rodriguez-Abreu_2016_p._369-15)

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3a/Cocktail_B52.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cocktail_B52.jpg)

The [B-52](/wiki/B-52_(cocktail) "B-52 (cocktail)") is a [layered drink](/wiki/Layered_drink "Layered drink") prepared using [Grand Marnier](/wiki/Grand_Marnier "Grand Marnier") atop [Irish cream](/wiki/Irish_cream "Irish cream") over a base of [coffee liqueur](/wiki/Coffee_liqueur "Coffee liqueur")

### Cocktails

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Cocktails")]

Adding liqueurs to a cocktail can change the flavour and appearance of the cocktail. Whilst some liqueurs are coloured and designed to make the cocktail pop in colour, others are clear to prevent the liqueur from taking over the colour of the base spirit or garnish.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Layered drinks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Layered drinks")]

[Layered drinks](/wiki/Layered_drink "Layered drink") are made by floating different-colored liqueurs in separate layers. Each liqueur is poured slowly into a glass over the back of a spoon or down a glass rod, so that the liquids of different densities remain unmixed, creating a striped effect.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Health concerns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liqueur&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Health concerns")]

Alcohol-based [sugar-sweetened beverages](/wiki/Sweetened_beverage "Sweetened beverage"), are closely linked to [heavy episodic drinking](/wiki/Heavy_episodic_drinking "Heavy episodic drinking") in adolescents.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
